div.note: MORALE REPORT.
CONFIDENTIAL.
Part I. MORALE IN GENERAL.
Morale in general is quite good. Troops have recovered frail their reactions to the end of the European war.
Part II. FACTORS AFFECTING MORALE. hhh 1. Discipline.
Unit and individual discipline is good and is reflected in general bearing and turn-out of men both on parade and walking out. hhh 2. Confidence.
(a) In Army Leaders and the War. The finish in Europe has given all ranks finer confidence in their 'leaders and there is high optimism that the Asiatic War will soon be over.
(b) Aims and Equipment. Very satisfactory.
(c) In Efficiency. Never higher. hhh 3. Mail.
All reports show that mail conditions are excellent with no complaints. hhh 4. Welfare and Entertainment.
(a) Living Conditions. Realized they are the best possible under the circumstances. i.e. Rather over-crowded due to limited billeting facilities. Rations are satisfactory.
(b) Recreation and Amusements. Mainly football, cricket, and swimming. Plenty of Unit transport is laid on for recreation and for getting to places of amusement.
(c) General. Bathing trips are appreciated and as much sports gear as possible is being purchased. hhh 5. Education arid A.B.C.A.

Little interest shown, except for same correspondence courses and courses in vocational training likely to be of use in civil life. Not much has been done on a unit basis owing to impending disbandment. The Post-hostilities Education Scheme is not very popular; probably further publicity will dispell this. hhh 6. Relations between Officers and Men.
Just as they should be a good and co-operative spirit. hhh 7. Relations with other services.
Satisfactory although not much contact. hhh 8. Relations with Allies.
Good, except with Yugoslavs owing to their recent change of attitude and enforcement of petty restrictions when our men have been working in Yugoslavia.
They are now regarded as being on the same level as Italians. hhh 9. Home Affairs, Post War and Release.
Not much interest in home affairs, except for forthcoming General Election, about which each man is expressing his individual political opinion.

Opinion rather confused about Post War affairs. After so much publicity and prophesy regarding the 'New World', most men prefer to dwell on more mundane matters. It is realised that a great deal depends on the San Francisco Conference. Some doubts expressed about the future of Anglo-Russian relations in view of recent events, but the view taken differs according to the political beliefs of the individual.

Release plan now accepted, and it is realised that it will take its time to work out. Some younger men, recently sent abroad, are concerned about Far East Service, as it means that they will be in the Army for about 7-8 years in all. hhh 10. Finance.
Quite satisfactory. Great majority of men have credits. hhh 11. Miscellaneous.

(a) Overseas Service. General hope that Python will be reduced. Some disappointment that it has not done so already. Large increase in compassionate posting applications, and S.S.A.F.A. interviews indicate that the length overseas service is making itself felt even among normal married people. The end of the European war has precipitated a number of matrimonial troubles which would have to come to a head sooner or later.

(b) Leave. Realised that LIAP is too remote for the ordinary man, and is only for the 'lucky' individual. Bitterness at B.L.A. personnel getting hone regularly. Rome and local leave facilities much appreciated.
(c) Relations with civil population. Little or no contact.
